  • Part 1: Data Preparation
    • Task 1A: Export 311 Data
    • Task 1B: Prepare to Join
  • Part 2: Join

  • Part 3: Filter to heating and hot water complaints

CUNY ISLG Data Skills Assessment – 311 Service Requests

Author

Richa Shiny Tigiripally

Published

April 2, 2026

#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code:"
#| label: libraries
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# These are the packages we need for this analysis
# tidyverse = data manipulation and visualization
# httr = making API calls to NYC Open Data
# scales = formatting numbers as percentages
# knitr = making nice tables in the output

library(tidyverse)
library(httr)
library(scales)
library(knitr)

Part 1: Data Preparation

Task 1A: Export 311 Data

I pulled the 311 data programmatically from the NYC Open Data Socrata API rather than exporting manually. This makes the pipeline fully reproducible — anyone running this notebook will pull the same data from the same source without needing a local file.

#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code:"
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| label: pull-311-data
#| cache: true

# The NYC Open Data API endpoint for 311 service requests
# We use the .csv format so we can read it directly into R with read_csv
base_url <- "https://data.cityofnewyork.us/resource/erm2-nwe9.csv"

# Build the query URL with three filters applied server-side:
# 1. borough = MANHATTAN (scope to Manhattan only)
# 2. created_date >= Jan 1 2024 (start of Q1)
# 3. created_date <= Mar 31 2024 (end of Q1)
# $limit=200000 overrides the default 1000-row cap from the API
query <- paste0(
  base_url,
  "?$where=",
  URLencode("borough='MANHATTAN' AND created_date >= '2024-01-01T00:00:00' AND created_date <= '2024-03-31T23:59:59'"),
  "&$limit=200000"
)

# Make the HTTP GET request to the API
response <- httr::GET(query)

# Extract the raw text content from the response
raw_text <- httr::content(response, as = "text", encoding = "UTF-8")

# Parse the CSV text into a dataframe
df_311_raw <- read_csv(raw_text, show_col_types = FALSE)

# Print how many rows and columns we got back
cat("Total rows pulled:", nrow(df_311_raw), "\n")
Total rows pulled: 165894 
cat("Total columns:", ncol(df_311_raw), "\n")
Total columns: 44

Task 1B: Prepare to Join

Before joining, we need to understand what the join key looks like in both datasets. The reference table uses integers 1–12 for community districts. The 311 data stores community_board as a string that needs cleaning.

#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code:"
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| label: load-reference

# Pull the community district reference CSV directly from GitHub
# This avoids needing a local copy of the file
cd_ref <- read_csv("manhattan_community_district_reference.csv",
                   show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
# We rename community_board to cd_numeric for two reasons:
# 1. The reference table's community_board is already clean integers (1-12)
# 2. The 311 community_board field is messy strings ("01", "0", "Unspecified").
# So we will clean the 311 side into a new column also called cd_numeric. Renaming both sides to the same name (cd_numeric) makes the join keyexplicit and shows the cleaning was intentional before joining
  rename(cd_numeric = community_board)

# Print the reference table to confirm it loaded correctly
kable(cd_ref, caption = "Community District Reference Table")
Community District Reference Table
cd_numeric district_name neighborhood planning_area total_units residential_units
1 Manhattan Community Board 1 Financial District / Tribeca / Civic Center Lower Manhattan 59201 47267
2 Manhattan Community Board 2 Greenwich Village / SoHo / NoHo / Little Italy Lower Manhattan 72751 61370
3 Manhattan Community Board 3 Lower East Side / Chinatown Lower Manhattan 94149 83639
4 Manhattan Community Board 4 Clinton / Hell’s Kitchen / Chelsea Midtown 102014 88333
5 Manhattan Community Board 5 Midtown / Flatiron / Gramercy Midtown 92470 45878
6 Manhattan Community Board 6 Stuyvesant Town / Kips Bay / Murray Hill Midtown 114930 103972
7 Manhattan Community Board 7 Upper West Side / Manhattan Valley Upper Manhattan 134534 128248
8 Manhattan Community Board 8 Upper East Side / Yorkville / Lenox Hill Upper Manhattan 159953 151425
9 Manhattan Community Board 9 Morningside Heights / West Harlem Upper Manhattan 49323 46846
10 Manhattan Community Board 10 Central Harlem Upper Manhattan 65710 61902
11 Manhattan Community Board 11 East Harlem Upper Manhattan 62798 59729
12 Manhattan Community Board 12 Washington Heights / Inwood / Marble Hill Upper Manhattan 79412 75860 
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code:"
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| label: inspect-311-join-key

# Before we can join, we need to understand what the community_board
# field looks like in the 311 data
# It is stored as a string and may have unexpected values

# Extract the trailing numeric portion from the community_board string
# For example "01" becomes 1, "12" becomes 12
# Values like "0" or "Unspecified" will become NA or 0
df_311 <- df_311_raw %>%
  mutate(
    # str_extract pulls the number at the end of the string
    # as.integer converts it from string to number
    cd_numeric = suppressWarnings(as.integer(str_extract(community_board, "\\d+$"))),
    
    # Flag whether this record has a valid Manhattan CD (1 through 12)
    # Records outside this range cannot be matched to the reference table
    cd_valid = !is.na(cd_numeric) & cd_numeric >= 1 & cd_numeric <= 12
  )

# Show the most common community_board values so we can see
# what we are working with before joining
df_311 %>%
  count(community_board, cd_numeric, cd_valid, sort = TRUE) %>%
  head(20) %>%
  kable(caption = "Top community_board values in 311 data before joining")
Top community_board values in 311 data before joining
community_board cd_numeric cd_valid n
12 MANHATTAN NA FALSE 25341
10 MANHATTAN NA FALSE 19490
07 MANHATTAN NA FALSE 16863
09 MANHATTAN NA FALSE 14155
03 MANHATTAN NA FALSE 14118
04 MANHATTAN NA FALSE 12539
05 MANHATTAN NA FALSE 12187
08 MANHATTAN NA FALSE 10870
06 MANHATTAN NA FALSE 10268
02 MANHATTAN NA FALSE 9833
11 MANHATTAN NA FALSE 9473
01 MANHATTAN NA FALSE 6320
Unspecified MANHATTAN NA FALSE 3530
08 BRONX NA FALSE 539
64 MANHATTAN NA FALSE 368

The join key is community_board, matched from the 311 data to the community_board field in the reference table (renamed to cd_numeric on both sides for clarity). Before joining, inspection of the 311 community_board field revealed that values are stored as strings in the format “12 MANHATTAN”, “07 MANHATTAN”, etc., with the borough name appended after the district number. This means a direct string match against the reference table’s integer values (1-12) would fail entirely. Additional unexpected values include “Unspecified MANHATTAN” for requests that were not geocoded to a specific district, and a small number of records like “08 BRONX” that passed the borough filter but belong to another borough. To prepare for the join, I extract the leading numeric portion from the 311 community_board string using a regex pattern (^\d+), cast it to integer, and validate that the result falls within the 1-12 range of Manhattan community districts. Records outside this range are retained in the dataset but will not match the reference table and are diagnosed in Task 2B.

#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code:"
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| label: fix-311-join-key

# Now that we know the format is "07 MANHATTAN" we extract from the START
# of the string using ^\\d+ which means "digits at the beginning"
# This correctly pulls 7 from "07 MANHATTAN", 12 from "12 MANHATTAN" etc.
df_311 <- df_311_raw %>%
  mutate(
    # Extract leading digits and convert to integer
    cd_numeric = suppressWarnings(as.integer(str_extract(community_board, "^\\d+"))),
    
    # Flag valid Manhattan CDs only (1 through 12)
    # Anything outside this range will not match the reference table
    cd_valid = !is.na(cd_numeric) & cd_numeric >= 1 & cd_numeric <= 12
  )

# Check the distribution again after the fix
df_311 %>%
  count(community_board, cd_numeric, cd_valid, sort = TRUE) %>%
  head(20) %>%
  kable(caption = "community_board values after cleaning")
community_board values after cleaning
community_board cd_numeric cd_valid n
12 MANHATTAN 12 TRUE 25341
10 MANHATTAN 10 TRUE 19490
07 MANHATTAN 7 TRUE 16863
09 MANHATTAN 9 TRUE 14155
03 MANHATTAN 3 TRUE 14118
04 MANHATTAN 4 TRUE 12539
05 MANHATTAN 5 TRUE 12187
08 MANHATTAN 8 TRUE 10870
06 MANHATTAN 6 TRUE 10268
02 MANHATTAN 2 TRUE 9833
11 MANHATTAN 11 TRUE 9473
01 MANHATTAN 1 TRUE 6320
Unspecified MANHATTAN NA FALSE 3530
08 BRONX 8 TRUE 539
64 MANHATTAN 64 FALSE 368

Part 2: Join

Task 2A: Perform the Join

#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code:"
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| label: join

# Perform a LEFT JOIN from the 311 data to the reference table
# We use a left join because we want to keep ALL 311 records
# including ones that do not match a community district
# An inner join would silently drop unmatched records
# A right join would drop unmatched 311 records entirely
df_joined <- df_311 %>%
  left_join(cd_ref, by = "cd_numeric")

# Confirm row count matches original
# A left join should never add or drop rows
cat("Rows before join:", nrow(df_311), "\n")
Rows before join: 165894 
cat("Rows after join:", nrow(df_joined), "\n")
Rows after join: 165894 
cat("Rows match:", nrow(df_311) == nrow(df_joined), "\n")
Rows match: TRUE 
# Preview the joined dataset
df_joined %>%
  select(unique_key, community_board, cd_numeric, cd_valid,
         neighborhood, planning_area, complaint_type) %>%
  head(10) %>%
  kable(caption = "Sample rows from joined dataset")
Sample rows from joined dataset
unique_key community_board cd_numeric cd_valid neighborhood planning_area complaint_type
60738841 08 MANHATTAN 8 TRUE Upper East Side / Yorkville / Lenox Hill Upper Manhattan HEAT/HOT WATER
60739109 03 MANHATTAN 3 TRUE Lower East Side / Chinatown Lower Manhattan Street Condition
60736744 08 MANHATTAN 8 TRUE Upper East Side / Yorkville / Lenox Hill Upper Manhattan Noise - Commercial
60738136 04 MANHATTAN 4 TRUE Clinton / Hell’s Kitchen / Chelsea Midtown Illegal Parking
60736024 09 MANHATTAN 9 TRUE Morningside Heights / West Harlem Upper Manhattan Noise - Street/Sidewalk
60734318 09 MANHATTAN 9 TRUE Morningside Heights / West Harlem Upper Manhattan HEAT/HOT WATER
60739040 04 MANHATTAN 4 TRUE Clinton / Hell’s Kitchen / Chelsea Midtown Illegal Parking
60738703 12 MANHATTAN 12 TRUE Washington Heights / Inwood / Marble Hill Upper Manhattan Noise - Residential
60735893 10 MANHATTAN 10 TRUE Central Harlem Upper Manhattan Noise - Residential
60735437 07 MANHATTAN 7 TRUE Upper West Side / Manhattan Valley Upper Manhattan Noise - Helicopter

Task 2B: Diagnose Non-Matches

#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code:"
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| label: non-matches

# Non-matches are rows where planning_area is NA after the join
# These are records that could not be matched to a community district
non_matches <- df_joined %>%
  filter(is.na(planning_area))

cat("Total non-matching records:", nrow(non_matches), "\n")
Total non-matching records: 3898 
cat("Share of total:", scales::percent(nrow(non_matches) / nrow(df_joined), 0.1), "\n")
Share of total: 2.3% 
# What community_board values do the non-matches have?
# This tells us WHY they did not match
non_matches %>%
  count(community_board, cd_numeric, sort = TRUE) %>%
  kable(caption = "community_board values among non-matching records")
community_board values among non-matching records
community_board cd_numeric n
Unspecified MANHATTAN NA 3530
64 MANHATTAN 64 368

Response to Task 2B: After joining, 3,898 records (2.3% of the dataset) did not match a row in the reference table. These non-matches fall into two categories: 1. “Unspecified MANHATTAN” (3,530 records) — Requests that were submitted but not geocoded to a specific community district. This is common for complaints called in by phone before an address is fully verified, or for requests where the location could not be resolved to a specific district at intake. 2. “64 MANHATTAN” (368 records) — Records with a community board value of 64, which falls outside the 1-12 range of Manhattan community districts. This appears to be a data entry or coding anomaly in the source system, as Manhattan only has 12 community districts. These records are retained in df_joined but excluded from the neighborhood-level analysis in Part 3, since they cannot be reliably assigned to a specific community board. I would not attempt to impute a community district from address fields without additional validation. The risk of misassignment outweighs the benefit given that non-matches represent only 2.3% of total records.

Part 3: Filter to heating and hot water complaints

#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code:"
#| label: heat-filter
#| echo: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# The exercise asks us to identify 311 calls related to heating or hot water
# We use the complaint_type field and search for "HEAT" or "HOT WATER"
# toupper() makes the match case-insensitive so we catch all variations
# We exclude non-matched records (planning_area is NA) because we need
# to assign complaints to specific community boards for the recommendation

df_heat <- df_joined %>%
  filter(
    str_detect(toupper(complaint_type), "HEAT|HOT WATER"),
    !is.na(planning_area)
  )

cat("Total heat/hot water complaints:", nrow(df_heat), "\n")
Total heat/hot water complaints: 23447 
# Show which complaint_type values were captured
# This is our answer to "which values did you use?" in Task 3
df_heat %>%
  count(complaint_type, sort = TRUE) %>%
  kable(caption = "Complaint types identified as heat or hot water related")
Complaint types identified as heat or hot water related
complaint_type n
HEAT/HOT WATER 23338
Non-Residential Heat 109 
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code:"
#| label: by-planning-area
#| echo: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Count complaints by planning area to determine which area has the greatest need
# This answers "which planning area?" in Task 3
df_heat %>%
  count(planning_area, sort = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(pct = scales::percent(n / sum(n), 0.1)) %>%
  kable(caption = "Heat and hot water complaints by planning area")
Heat and hot water complaints by planning area
planning_area n pct
Upper Manhattan 18691 79.7%
Midtown 2496 10.6%
Lower Manhattan 2260 9.6% 
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code:"
#| label: by-cd-normalized
#| echo: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Count complaints by planning area and community board
# Then normalize by residential units to account for differences in
# housing density across districts
# Raw counts alone would favor larger districts simply because they have
# more housing units, not because conditions are worse
df_heat %>%
  count(planning_area, cd_numeric, neighborhood) %>%
  left_join(cd_ref %>% select(cd_numeric, residential_units), by = "cd_numeric") %>%
  mutate(
    # Complaints per 1000 residential units gives a fair comparison
    # across districts of different sizes
    per_1000 = round(n / residential_units * 1000, 1)
  ) %>%
  arrange(planning_area, desc(per_1000)) %>%
  kable(caption = "Heat and hot water complaints per 1,000 residential units by community board")
Heat and hot water complaints per 1,000 residential units by community board
planning_area cd_numeric neighborhood n residential_units per_1000
Lower Manhattan 3 Lower East Side / Chinatown 1309 83639 15.7
Lower Manhattan 2 Greenwich Village / SoHo / NoHo / Little Italy 752 61370 12.3
Lower Manhattan 1 Financial District / Tribeca / Civic Center 199 47267 4.2
Midtown 6 Stuyvesant Town / Kips Bay / Murray Hill 1171 103972 11.3
Midtown 4 Clinton / Hell’s Kitchen / Chelsea 904 88333 10.2
Midtown 5 Midtown / Flatiron / Gramercy 421 45878 9.2
Upper Manhattan 12 Washington Heights / Inwood / Marble Hill 6634 75860 87.5
Upper Manhattan 10 Central Harlem 4349 61902 70.3
Upper Manhattan 9 Morningside Heights / West Harlem 3114 46846 66.5
Upper Manhattan 11 East Harlem 1640 59729 27.5
Upper Manhattan 7 Upper West Side / Manhattan Valley 1695 128248 13.2
Upper Manhattan 8 Upper East Side / Yorkville / Lenox Hill 1259 151425 8.3 
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code:"
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| label: chart
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 5

# Bar chart showing complaints per 1,000 residential units by community board
# Colored by planning area so the pattern is immediately visible
df_heat %>%
  count(planning_area, cd_numeric, neighborhood) %>%
  left_join(cd_ref %>% select(cd_numeric, residential_units), by = "cd_numeric") %>%
  mutate(
    per_1000 = round(n / residential_units * 1000, 1),
    neighborhood = fct_reorder(neighborhood, per_1000)
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = per_1000, y = neighborhood, fill = planning_area)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = per_1000), hjust = -0.2, size = 3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Lower Manhattan" = "#2c7bb6",
    "Midtown"         = "#abd9e9",
    "Upper Manhattan" = "#d7191c"
  )) +
  labs(
    title   = "Heating and Hot Water 311 Complaints per 1,000 Residential Units",
    subtitle = "Manhattan Community Districts, Q1 2024",
    x       = "Complaints per 1,000 residential units",
    y       = NULL,
    fill    = "Planning Area",
    caption = "Source: NYC 311 Open Data, Q1 2024"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

Response to Part 3: Which service calls? I identified complaints using the complaint_type field, filtering for values containing “HEAT” or “HOT WATER.” This captured two types: HEAT/HOT WATER (23,338 records) and Non-Residential Heat (109 records), totaling 23,447 complaints. Which planning area? I recommend Upper Manhattan. It accounts for 18,691 complaints, or 79.7% of all heat and hot water complaints in Manhattan during Q1 2024. This concentration holds when normalized by residential units, with Upper Manhattan districts occupying the top four spots citywide. Which community boards? Within Upper Manhattan, I recommend targeting:

CB 12, Washington Heights / Inwood / Marble Hill at 87.5 complaints per 1,000 residential units, the highest rate in Manhattan by a significant margin. CB 10, Central Harlem at 70.3 complaints per 1,000 residential units, the second highest rate overall.

Both districts have large shares of older rent-stabilized and low-income housing where tenants are most dependent on landlord-provided heat and most likely to benefit from outreach and education about their rights. Limitations: 311 data measures reporting, not the underlying prevalence of heating problems. Communities with lower 311 engagement due to language barriers, distrust of city systems, or lack of awareness may appear to have fewer complaints even if conditions are equally poor. This analysis is limited to Q1 2024, which captures the peak heating season and may not reflect conditions year-round. Residential unit counts used for normalization are static reference figures and may not reflect current occupancy. Approximately 2.3% of records could not be assigned to a community district and are excluded from these counts.

#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code:"
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| label: chart-heatmap
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 5

# Chart 2: Heatmap of complaints by community board and month
# This shows both WHICH districts have the most complaints
# AND whether the problem is consistent across all three months
# or concentrated in a single cold snap

df_heat %>%
  mutate(month = lubridate::month(created_date, label = TRUE)) %>%
  count(neighborhood, month) %>%
  mutate(neighborhood = fct_reorder(neighborhood, n, sum)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = month, y = neighborhood, fill = n)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(n)), size = 3) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#fff5f0", high = "#d7191c", labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(
    title    = "Heating and Hot Water Complaints by Community Board and Month",
    subtitle = "Manhattan, Q1 2024",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = NULL,
    fill     = "Complaints",
    caption  = "Source: NYC 311 Open Data, Q1 2024"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code:"
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| label: chart-monthly-trend
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 5

# Chart 3: Monthly trend of heat complaints by planning area
# Shows whether complaints are concentrated in January (coldest month)
# or spread across the quarter
# This gives HPD context on when outreach would be most impactful

df_heat %>%
  mutate(month = lubridate::month(created_date, label = TRUE)) %>%
  count(month, planning_area) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = month, y = n, color = planning_area, group = planning_area)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(n)), vjust = -0.8, size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Lower Manhattan" = "#2c7bb6",
    "Midtown"         = "#abd9e9",
    "Upper Manhattan" = "#d7191c"
  )) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(
    title    = "Monthly Heating and Hot Water Complaints by Planning Area",
    subtitle = "Manhattan, Q1 2024",
    x        = "Month",
    y        = "Number of complaints",
    color    = "Planning Area",
    caption  = "Source: NYC 311 Open Data, Q1 2024"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code:"
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| label: chart-agency
#| echo: true
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 5

# Chart 4: Which agencies are handling heat complaints in Upper Manhattan
# Since we are advising HPD specifically, this shows what share of
# heat complaints in our recommended area are already in HPD's purview
# versus other agencies

df_heat %>%
  filter(planning_area == "Upper Manhattan") %>%
  count(agency_name, sort = TRUE) %>%
  head(5) %>%
  mutate(agency_name = fct_reorder(agency_name, n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = agency_name)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#d7191c") +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(n)), hjust = -0.2, size = 3) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Agencies Handling Heat and Hot Water Complaints in Upper Manhattan",
    subtitle = "Top 5 agencies, Q1 2024",
    x        = "Number of complaints",
    y        = NULL,
    caption  = "Source: NYC 311 Open Data, Q1 2024"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
#| code-fold: true
#| code-summary: "Show code:"
#| label: chart-map
#| echo: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(leaflet)

# Approximate lat/lon centroids for each Manhattan community board
# These are used to position the bubbles on the map
cd_coords <- tibble(
  cd_numeric = 1:12,
  lat = c(40.714, 40.730, 40.715, 40.753, 40.745, 40.744,
          40.784, 40.774, 40.811, 40.818, 40.796, 40.851),
  lon = c(-74.008, -74.002, -73.991, -74.002, -73.986, -73.973,
          -73.981, -73.957, -73.954, -73.938, -73.938, -73.937)
)

# Join complaint counts and normalized rates to the coordinates
map_data <- df_heat %>%
  count(cd_numeric, neighborhood, planning_area) %>%
  left_join(cd_ref %>% select(cd_numeric, residential_units), by = "cd_numeric") %>%
  mutate(per_1000 = round(n / residential_units * 1000, 1)) %>%
  left_join(cd_coords, by = "cd_numeric")

# Color palette by planning area
pal <- colorFactor(
  palette = c("#2c7bb6", "#abd9e9", "#d7191c"),
  levels  = c("Lower Manhattan", "Midtown", "Upper Manhattan")
)

# Build the interactive bubble map
# Bubble size = raw complaint count
# Bubble color = planning area
# Popup shows neighborhood name, raw count, and rate per 1,000 units
leaflet(map_data) %>%
  addProviderTiles(providers$CartoDB.Positron) %>%
  addCircleMarkers(
    lng    = ~lon,
    lat    = ~lat,
    radius = ~sqrt(n) / 3,
    color  = ~pal(planning_area),
    fillColor = ~pal(planning_area),
    fillOpacity = 0.7,
    stroke = TRUE,
    weight = 1.5,
    popup  = ~paste0(
      "<b>", neighborhood, "</b><br>",
      "Planning Area: ", planning_area, "<br>",
      "Total complaints: ", scales::comma(n), "<br>",
      "Per 1,000 units: ", per_1000
    )
  ) %>%
  addLegend(
    position = "bottomright",
    pal      = pal,
    values   = ~planning_area,
    title    = "Planning Area"
  )
